scenario: Apex Healthcare validates 3000 records per day in Camunda 8.8 using a FEEL rule on a business rule task. The rule must determine whether a patient identifier string matches a regular expression in a case insensitive manner, so the comparison ignores letter casing differences between the identifier and the configured pattern.
question: Which FEEL matches call returns true when the input matches the pattern under case insensitive comparison?

options.text: matches(input, pattern, "s") returns true when the input matches with the dot all flag enabled across newline characters
matches(input, pattern, "m") returns true when the input matches with the multi line flag enabled at line boundaries
matches(input, pattern, "x") returns true when the input matches with the comments flag enabled inside the regular expression
matches(input, pattern, "i") returns true when the input matches with the case insensitive flag enabled during the comparison

a.text: Incorrect. The s flag enables dot-all mode so the . metacharacter matches newline characters; it does not change letter casing behavior of the comparison.

b.text: Incorrect. The m flag enables multi-line mode so anchors match at internal line boundaries; it does not enable case insensitive matching of the input.

c.text: Incorrect. The x flag enables comments mode inside the regular expression; it does not affect case sensitivity of the comparison between input and pattern.

d.text: Correct. The i flag in matches(input, pattern, flags) enables case insensitive comparison, so matches("FooBar", "foo", "i") returns true under the documented FEEL behavior.

explanation: matches(input, pattern, flags) returns true when input matches the regular expression pattern. The flags string can include s (dot-all), m (multi-line), i (case insensitive), and x (comments).
